quab: A fish of Russian rivers, which delights in clear water.
quachilto: A Brazilian fowl of the moor-hen kind, of a fine black color variegated with white. It voice resembles the crowing of a cock.
quack: To cry like a duck or goose.
To boast; to bounce; to talk noisily and ostentatiously; as, pretenders to medical skill quack of their cures.
A boaster; one who pretends to skill or knowledge which he does not possess.
A boastful pretender to medical skill which he does not possess; an empiric; an ignorant practitioner.
quackery: The boastful pretensions or mean practice of an ignoramus, particularly in medicine; empiricism.
quackish: Like a quack; boasting of skill not possessed; trickish.
quacksalver: One who boasts of his skill in medicines and salves, or of the efficacy of his prescriptions; a charlatan.
quadragene: A papal indulgence multiplying remissions by forties.
quadragesima: Lent; so called because it consists of forty days.
quadragesimal: Belonging to Lent; used in Lent.
quadragesimals: Offerings formerly made to the mother church on mid-lent Sunday.
quadrangle: In geometry, a quadrilateral figure; a square; a figure consisting of four sides and four angles.
quadrangular: Square; having four sides and four angles.
In botany, having four prominent angles, as a stem or leaf.
quadrant: In geometry, the quarter of a circle; the arc of a circle containing ninety degrees; also, the space or area included between this arc and two radii drawn from the center to each extremity.

An instrument for taking the altitudes of the sun or stars, of great use in astronomy and navigation. Quadrants are variously made, but they all consist of the quarter of a circle whose limb is divided into ninety degrees; or, as in Hadley’s reflecting quadrant, an arc of forty five degrees is made to serve the same purpose as an arc of ninety degrees. Quadrant of altitude, an appendage of the artificial globe, consisting of a slip of brass of the length of a quadrant of one of the great circles of the globe, and graduated. It is filled to the meridian and movable round to all points of the horizon. It serves as a scale in measuring altitudes, azimuthsIMAD .
quadrantal: Pertaining to a quadrant; also, included in the fourth part of a circle; as quadrantal space.
A vessel used by the Romans; originally called amphora. It was square and contained 80 pounds of water.
quadrat: In printing, a piece of metal used to fill the void spaces between wordsQuadrats are of different sizes; as m-quadrats.
A mathematical instrument, called also a geometrical square, and line of shadows.
quadrate: Square; having four equal and parallel sides.
A square; a surface with four equal and parallel sides.
quadratic: Square; denoting a square or pertaining to it. Quadratic equation, in algebra, an equation in which the unknown quantity is of two dimensions, or raised to the second power; or one in which the highest power of the unknown quantity is a square.
quadratrix: In geometry, a mechanical line by means of which we can find right lines equal to the circumference of circles or other curves and their several parts.
quadrature: The act of squaring; the reducing of a figure to a square. Thus the finding of a square which shall contain just as much area as a circle or a triangle, is the quadrature of that circle or triangle.

In astronomy, the aspect of the moon when distant from the sun 90 degrees or a quarter of the circle; or when the moon is at an equal distance from the points of conjunction and opposition. Quadrature of curves, in mathematics, the finding of rectilineal figures containing the same areas as figures bounded by curved lines.
quadrel: In architecture, a kind of artificial stone made of chalky earth and dried in the shade for two years; so called from being square.
quadrennial: Comprising four years; as a quadrennial period.
Occurring once in four years as quadrennial games.
quadricapsular: In botany, having four capsules to a flower; as a quadricapsular pericarp.
quadridecimal: In crystallography, designating a crystal whose prism or the middle part has four faces and two summits, containing together ten faces.
quadridentate: In botany, having four teeth on the edge.
quadrifid: In botany, four-cleft, as a quadrifid perianth; cut into four segments, with linear sinuses and straight margins, as a quadrifid leaf.
quadrijugous: In botany, pinnate, with four pairs of leaflets; as a quadrijugous leaf.
quadrilateral: Having four sides and four angles.
A figure having four sides and four angles; a quadrangular figure.
quadrilateralness: The property of having four right lined sides, forming as many right angles.
quadrille: A game played by four persons with 40 cards, being the remainder of the pack after the four tens, nines and eights are discarded.
quadrilobate: In botany, having four lobes; as a quadrilobed leaf.
quadrilobed: In botany, having four lobes; as a quadrilobed leaf.
quadrilocular: Having four cells; four-celled; as a quadrilocular pericarp.
quadrin: A mite; a small piece of money, in value about a farthing.
quadrinomial: Consisting of four denominations or terms.
quadripartite: Divided into four parts, or consisting of four corresponding parts.
quadripartitely: In four divisions; in a quadripartite distribution.
quadripartition: A division by four or into four parts; or the taking the fourth part of any quantity or number.
quadrireme: A galley with four benches of oars or rowers.
quadrisyllable: A word consisting of four syllables.
quadrivalve: In botany, having four valves; four-valved; as a quadrivalve pericarp.
quadrivalvular: In botany, having four valves; four-valved; as a quadrivalve pericarp.
quadrivalves: A door with four folds or leaves.
quadrivial: Having four ways meeting in a point.
quadroon: In Spanish America, the offspring of a mulatto woman by a white man; a person quarter-blooded.
quadruman: An animal having four hands or limbs that correspond to the hands of a man, as a monkey.
quadrune: A gritstone with a calcarious cement.
quadruped: An animal having four legs and feet, as a horse, an ox, a lion
quadruple: Fourfold; four times told; as, to make quadruple restitution for trespass or theft.
Four times the sum or number; as, to receive quadruple the amount in damages or profits.
quadruplicate: Fourfold; four times repeated; as a quadruplicate ratio or proportion.
To make fourfold; to double twice.
quadruplication: The act of making fourfold and taking four times the simple sum or amount.
quadruply: To a fourfold quantity; as, to be quadruply recompensed.
quaere: Inquire; better written query, which see.
quaff: To drink; to swallow in large draughts. He quaffs the muscadel. They in communion sweet quaff immortality and joy.
quaffer: One that quaffs or drinks largely.
quaggy: Yielding to the feet or trembling under the foot, as soft wet earth.
quagmire: Soft wet land, which has a surface firm enough to bear a person, but which shakes or yields under the feet.
quahaug: quaw’hog. In New England, the popular name of a large species of clams or bivalvular shells.
quaid: or pp. Crushed, subdued, or depressed.
quail: To sink into dejection; to languish; to fail in spirits.
To curdle; to coagulate; as milk.
To crush; to depress; to sink; to subdue.

A bird of the genus Tetrao or grouse kind, or according to Latham’s arrangement, of the genus Perdix, in which he comprehends the partridge and quail. In New England, the name is applied to a peculiar species of the perdix, which is called partridge in the middie states, but it is neither the partridge nor quail of Europe.
quailing: The act of failing in spirit or resolution; decay. Obs.
quail-pipe: A pipe or call for alluring quails into a net; a kind of leathern purse in the shape of a pear, partly filled with horse hair, with a whistle at the end.
quaint: Nice; scrupulously and superfluously exact; having petty elegance; as a quaint phrase; a quaint fashion. To show how quaint an orator you are.
In common use, odd; fanciful; singular; and so used by Chaucer.
quaintly: Nicely; exactly; with petty neatness or spruceness; as hair more quaintly curled.
Artfully . Breathe his faults so quaintly.
Ingeniously; with dexterity. I quaintly stole a kiss.
quaintness: Niceness; petty neatness or elegance. There is a majesty in simplicity, which is far above the quaintness of wit.
quake: To shake; to tremble; to be agitated with quick but short motions continually repeated; to shudder. Thus we say, a person quakes with fear or terror, or with cold. Hebrews 12:21.
To shake with violent convulsions, as well as with trembling; as, the earth quakes; the mountains quake. Nahum 1:5.
To shake, tremble or move, as the earth under the feet; as the quaking mud.
To frighten; to throw into agitation.
A shake; a trembling; a shudder; a tremulous agitation.
quaker: One that quakes; but usually, one of the religious sect called friends. This name, quakers, is said to have been given to the sect in reproach, an account of some agitations which distinguished them; but it is no longer appropriated to them by way of reproach.
quakerism: The peculiar manners, tenets or worship of the quakers.
quaking: A shaking; tremulous agitation; trepidation. Daniel 10:7.
qualifiable: That may be qualified; that may be abated or modified.
qualification: Any natural endowment or any acquirement which fits a person for a place, office or employment, or enables him to sustain any character with success. Integrity and talents should be considered as indispensable qualifications for men entrusted with public affairs; but private interest and party-spirit will often dispense with these and all other qualifications. There is no qualification for government but virtue and wisdom, actual or presumptive.
Legal power or requisite; as the qualifications of electors.
Modification; restriction; limitation. words or expressions may be used in a general sense, without any qualification.
qualified: Fitted by accomplishments or endowments; modified. Qualified fee, in law, a base fee, or an estate which has a qualification annexed to it, and which ceases with the qualification, as a grant to A and his heirs, tenants of the manor of Dale. Qualified negative, in legislation, the power of negativing bills which have passed the two houses of the legislature; a power vested in the president, governor or other officer, but subject to be overruled and defeated by a subsequent vote of the two houses, passed in conformity with the provisions of the constitution. Qualified property, is that which depends on temporary possession, as that in wild animals reclaimed.
qualifiedness: The state of being qualified or fitted.
qualifier: He or that which qualifies; that which modifies, reduces, tempers or restrains.
qualify: To fit for any place, office, occupation or character; to furnish with the knowledge, skill or other accomplishment necessary for a purpose; as, to qualify a man for a judge, for a minister of state or of the gospel, for a general or admiral. Holiness alone can qualify men for the society of holy beings.
To make capable of any employment or privilege; to furnish with legal power or capacity; as, in England, to qualify a man to kill game.
To abate; to soften; to diminish; as, to qualify the rigor of a statute. I do no seek to quench your love’s hot fire, but qualify the fire’s extreme rage.
To modify; to restrain; to limit by exceptions; as, to qualify words or expressions, or to qualify the sense of words or phrases.
qualifying: Furnishing with the necessary qualities, properties or accomplishments for a place, station or business; furnishing with legal power; abating; tempering; modifying; restraining.
quality: Property; that which belongs to a body or substance, or can be predicated of it. Qualities are natural or accidental. thus whiteness is a natural quality of snow; softness is a natural quality of wool and fur; hardness is a natural quality of metals and wood; figure and dimension are the natural qualities of solids; but a particular figure, as a cube, a square or a sphere, is an accidental or adventitious quality. The fluidity of metals is an accidental quality. Essential qualities are such as are necessary to constitute a thing what it is. Sensible qualities are such as are perceptible to the senses, as the light of the sun, the color of cloth, the taste of salt or sugar.
Nature, relatively considered; as the quality of an action, in regard to right and wrong. Other creatures have not judgment to examine the quality of that which is done by them.
Virtue or particular power of producing certain effects; as the qualities of plants or medicines.
Disposition; temper. Tonight we'll wander through the streets, and note the qualities of people.
Virtue or vice; as good qualities, or bad qualities.
qualm: A rising in the stomach, as it is commonly called; a fit of nausea, or a disposition or effort of the stomach to eject its contents.
A sudden fit or seizure of sickness at the stomach; a sensation of nausea; as qualms of heart-sickagony. QUALIM. For who, without a qualm, hath ever look’d on holy garbage, though by Homer cook’d?
A scruple of conscience, or uneasiness of conscience.
qualmish: Sick at the stomach; inclined to vomit; affected with nausea or sicklylanguor.
quamoclit: A plant of the genus Ipomoea.
quandary: Doubt; uncertainty; a state of difficulty or perplexity.
To bring into a state of uncertainty or difficulty.
quantity: That property of any thing which may be increased or diminished. This definition is defective, and as applicable to many other properties as to quantity. A definition strictly philosophical cannot be given. In common usage, quantity is a mass or collection of matter of indeterminate dimensions, but consisting of particles which cannot be distinguished, or which are not customarily distinguished, or which are considered in the aggregate. Thus we say, a quantity of earth, a quantity of water, a quantity of air, of light, of heat, of iron, of wood, of timber, of corn, of paper. But we do not say, a quantity of men, or of horses, or of houses; for as these are considered as separate individuals or beings, we call an assemblage of them, a number of multitude.
A portion or part. If I were sawed into quantities.
a large portion; as a medicine taken in quantities, that is, in large quantities.

In mathematics, any thing which can be multiplied, divided or measured. Thus mathematics is called the science of quantity. In algebra, quantities are known and unknown. Known quantities are usually represented by the first letters of the alphabet, as a, b, c, and unknown quantities are expressed by the last letters, x, y, zLetters thus used to represent quantities are themselves called quantities. A simple quantity is expressed by one term, as + a, or - abc; a compound is expressed by more terms than one, connected by the signs, + plus, or -minus, as a + b, or a - b + c. quantities which have the sign + prefixed, are called positive or affirmative; those which have the sign - prefixed are called negative.
quantum: The quantity; the amount. Quantum meruit, in law, an action grounded on a promise that the defendant would pay to the plaintiff for his service as much as he should deserve. Quantum valebat, an action to recover of the defendant for goods sold, as much as they were worth.
quarantine: Properly, the space of forty days; appropriately, the term of forty days during which a ship arriving in port and suspected of being infected with a malignant, contagious disease, is obliged to forbear all intercourse with the city or place. Hence,:

Restraint of intercourse to which a ship is subjected on the presumption that she may be infected, either for forty days or for any other limited term. It is customary for the proper officers to determine the period of restraint at their discretion, according to circumstances. Hence we hear of a quarantine of five days, of ten, of thirtyas well as of forty. We say, a ship performs quarantine, or rides at quarantine. We also apply the word to persons. The passengers and crew perform quarantine.
In law, the period of forty days, during which the widow of a man dying seized of land, has the privilege of remaining to the mansion house.

é. To prohibit from intercourse with a city or its inhabitants; to compel to remain at a distance from shore for forty days, or for other limited period, on account of real or supposed infection; applied to ships, or to persons and goods.
quarantined: Restrained from communication with the shore for a limited period; as a ship or its crew and passengers.
quarantining: Prohibiting from intercourse with the port; as a ship or its crew and passengers.
quarrel: A brawl; a petty fight or scuffle; from its noise and uproar.
A dispute; a contest. On open seas their quarrels they debate.
A breach of friendship or concord; open variance between parties.
Cause of dispute. The king’s quarrel is honorable.
Something that gives a right to mischief, reprisal or action. He thought he had a good quarrel to attack him.
quarreler: One who quarrels, wrangles or fights.
quarreling: Disputing with vehemence or loud angry words; scolding; wrangling; fighting; finding fault; disagreeing.
Contention; dispute in angry words; breach of concord; a caviling or finding fault; disagreement.
quarrelous: Apt or disposed to quarrel; petulant; easily provoked to enmity or contention.
quarrelsome: Apt to quarrel; given to brawls and contention; inclined to petty fighting; easily irritated or provoked to contest; irascible; choleric; petulant.
quarrelsomely: In a quarrelsome manner; with a quarrelsome temper; petulantly.
quarrelsomeness: Disposition to engage in contention and brawls; petulance.
quarried: Dug from a pit or cavern.
quarry: A square; as a quarry of glass.
An arrow with a square head.
In falconry, the game which a hawk is pursuing or has killed.
Among hunters, a part of the entrails of the beast taken, given to the hounds.

A place, cavern or pit where stones are dug from the earth, or separated from a large mass of rocks. We generally apply the word mine to the pit from which are taken metals and coal; from quarries are taken stones for building, as marble, freestone, slate.
quarryman: A man who is occupied in quarrying stones.
quart: The fourth part of a gallon; two pints.
A vessel containing the fourth of a gallon.
A sequence of four cards in the game of picket.
quartan: quort’an. Designating the fourth; occurring every fourth day; as a quartan ague or fever.
An intermitting ague that occurs every fourth day, or with intermissions of seventy two hours.
A measure containing the fourth part of some other measure.
quartation: In chimistry and metallurgy, the operation by which the quantity of one thing is made equal to the fourth part of another thing.
quarter: The fourth part; as the quarter of an hour or of a mile; one quarter of the expense. Living is a quarter dearer in the city than in the country.
In weight, the fourth part of a hundred pounds avoirdupois, or of 112 Ib., that is, 28 lb.; as a quarter of sugar.
In dry measure, the fourth of a ton in weight, or eight bushels; as a quarter of wheat.
In astronomy, the fourth part of the moon’s period or monthly revolution; as the first quarter after the change or full.
quarter-day: The day that completes three months, the quarter of a year; the day when quarterly payments are made of rent or interest.
quartered: Divided into four equal parts or quarters; separated into distinct parts; lodged; stationed for lodging.
quartering: Dividing into quarters or into distinct parts; stationing for lodgings.
The division of a shield containing many coats.
quarterly: Containing or consisting of a fourth part; as quarterly seasons.
Recurring at the end of each quarter of the year; as quarterly payments of rent; a quarterly visitation or examination. The secretary requires quarterly returns from his officers.
Once in a quarter of a year. The returns are made quarterly.
quartern: The fourth part of a pint; a gill.
quartile: An aspect of the planets, when they are distant from each other a quarter of the circle, ninety degrees, or three signs.
quarto: A book of the size of the fourth of a sheet; a size made by twice folding a sheet, which then makes four leaves.
Denoting the size of a book in which a sheet makes four leaves.
quartz: A species of silicious minerals, of various colors, white, gray, reddish, yellowish or brownish; commonly amorphous, and frequently crystallized. The subspecies and varieties are numerous.
quartzy: Pertaining to quartz; partaking of the nature or qualities of quartz; resembling quartz.
quas: In Russia, a drink of common domestic use; being a liquor prepared from pollard, meal and bread, or from meal and malt, by an acid fermentation.
quash: Properly, to beat down or beat in pieces; to crush. The whales against sharp rocks, like reeling vessels,
To be shaken with a noise.
A species of cucurbita; but in America pronounced squash; so called probably from it softness.
quassation: The act of shaking; concussion; the state of being shaken.
quassia: A plant, or rather a genus of plants of three species, the amara, simaruba, and excelsa or polygama, natives of South America and of some of the isles of the West Indies, and possessing valuable medicinal qualities.
quater-cousins: ka’ter-cuzns. Those within the first four degrees of kindred.
quatern: Consisting of four; fourfold; growing by fours; as quatern leaves.
querimoniousness: Disposition to complain; a complaining temper.
querist: One who inquires or asks questions.
querl: To twirl; to turn or wind round; to coil; as, to querl a cord, thread or rope.
quern: A hand-mill for grinding grain; a mill, the stone of which was turned by hand, used before the invention of windmills and watermills.
querpo: A waistcoat or garment close to the body.
querquedule: An aquatic fowl, a species of teal of the genus Anas.
querulous: Complaining or habitually complaining; disposed to murmur; as a querulous man or people.
Expressing complaint; as a querulous tone of voice.
querulousness: Disposition to complain, or the habit or practice of murmuring.
query: A question; an inquiry to be answered or resolved. I will conclude by proposing some queries.
To ask a question or questions. Three Cambridge sophs each prompt to query, answer and debate.
To seek; to inquire; as, query the sum or amount; query the motive or the fact.
quest: The act of seeking; search; as, to rove in quest of game; to go in quest of a lost child; in quest of property.
Request; desire; solicitation. Gad not abroad at every quest and call of an untrain’d hope or passion.
question: noun
ques’chun.
The act of asking; an interrogatory; as, to examine by question and answer.
That which is asked; something proposed which is to be solved by answer. What is the question?
Inquiry; disquisition; discussion. It is to be put to question, whether it is lawful for christian princes to make an invasive war, simply for the propagation of the faith.
Dispute or subject of debate. There arose a question between some of John’s disciples and the Jews, about purifying. John 3:25.
questionable: That may be questioned; doubtful; uncertain; disputable. the deed is of questionable authority. It is questionable whether Galen ever saw the dissection of a human body.
Suspicious; liable to be doubted or disputed; liable to suspicion. His veracity is questionable. Thou com’st in such a questionable shape, that I will speak to thee.
questionableness: The quality or state of being doubtful, questionable or suspicious.
questionary: Inquiring; asking questions; as questionary epistles.
questioner: One that asks questions; an inquirer.
questionless: Beyond a question or doubt; doubtless; certainly.
questman: A starter of lawsuits or prosecutions.
questmonger: A starter of lawsuits or prosecutions.
questor: In Roman antiquity, an officer who had the management of the public treasure; the receiver of taxes, tribute.
questorship: The office of a questor or Roman treasurer.
The term of a questor’s office.
quib: A sarcasm; a bitter taunt; a quip; a gibe.
quibble: A start or turn from the point in question, or from plain truth; an evasion; a cavil; a pretense; as, to answer a sound argument by quibbles. Quirks and quibbles have no place in the search after truth.
To evade the point in question, or plain truth, by artifice, play upon words, caviling or any conceit; to trifle in argument or discourse.
quibbler: One who evades plain truth by trifling artifices, play upon words, or cavils.
quick: Primarily, alive; living; opposed to dead or unanimated; as quick flesh. Leviticus 13:10. The Lord Jesus Christ, who shall judge the quick and the dead. Timothy 4:1.
Swift; hasty; done with celerity; as quick dispatch.
Speedy; done or occurring in a short time; as a quick return of profits. Oft he to her his charge of quick return repeated.
Active; brisk; nimble; prompt ready. He is remarkably quick in his motions. He is a man of quick parts.
quick-beam: A tree, the wild sorb, a species of wild ash. The Sorbus aucuparia, or mountain ash, a species of service tree.
quicken-tree: A tree, the wild sorb, a species of wild ash. The Sorbus aucuparia, or mountain ash, a species of service tree.
quicken: Primarily, to make alive; to vivify; to revive or resuscitate, as from death or an inanimate state. Romans 4:17. Hence flocks and herds, and men and beasts and fowls, with breath are quicken’d and attract their souls.
To make alive in a spiritual sense; to communicate a principle of grace to. You hath he quickened, who were dead in trespasses and sins. Ephesians 2:1.
To hasten; to accelerate; as, to quicken motion, speed or flight.
To sharpen; to give keener perception to; to stimulate; to incite; as, to quicken the appetite or taste; to quicken desires.
To revive; to cheer; to reinvigorate; to refresh by new supplies of comfort or grace. Psalm 119:25.
quickener: One who revives, vivifies, or communicates life.
That which accelerates motion or increases activity.
quick-eyed: Having acute sight; of keen and ready perception.
quicklime: Any calcarious substance deprived of its fixed or carbonic air, or an earthy substance calcined; as chalk, limestone, oyster-shells; unslaked lime. Calcarious stones and shells are reduced to quicklime by being subjected for a considerable time to intense heat, which expels the carbonic and aqeuous matter.
quick-match: A combustible preparation formed of cotton strands dipped in a boiling composition of white vinegar, saltpeter and mealed powder; used by artillerymen.
quickness: Speed; velocity; celerity; rapidity; as the quickness of motion.
Activity; briskness; promptness, as the quickness of the imagination or wit.
Acuteness of perception; keep sensibility; as quickness of sensation.
quicksand: Sand easily moved or readily yielding to pressure, loose sand abounding with water.
quickscented: Having an acute perception by the nose; of an acute smell.
quickset: A living plant set to grow, particularly for a hedge.
To plant with living shrubs or trees for a hedge or fence; as, to quickset a ditch.
quick-sighted: Having quick sight or acute discernment; quick to see or discern.
quicksightedness: Quickness of sight or discernment; readiness to see or discern.
quicksilver: Mercury, a metal found both native and in the state of ore, in mines, in various parts of the world, and so remarkably fusible as to be congealable only with the intense cold indicated by 39 degrees or 40 degrees below zero, on Fahrenheit’s thermometer. It is the heaviest of the metals, next to platina and gold. It is used in various arts and in medicine.
quid: A vulgar pronunciation of cud; as a quid of tobacco.
quiddany: Marmalade; a confection of quinces prepared with sugar.
quiddative: Constituting the essence of a thing.
quiddity: A barbarous term used in school philosophy for essence, that unknown and undefinable something which constitutes its peculiar nature, or answers the question, quidest? The essence of a thing constitutes it tale quid, such a thing as it is, and not another.
A trifling nicety; a cavil; a captious question.
quidnuncg: One who is curious to know every thing that passes; one who knows or pretends to know all occurrences.
quiesce: quiess’. To be silent, as a letter; to have no sound.
quiescence: Rest; repose; state of a thing without motion.
Rest of the mind; a state of the mind free from agitation or emotion.
Silence; the having no sound; as of a letter.
quiescency: Rest; repose; state of a thing without motion.
Rest of the mind; a state of the mind free from agitation or emotion.
Silence; the having no sound; as of a letter.
quiescent: Resting being in a state of repose; still: not moving; as a quiescent body or fluid.
Not ruffled with passion; unagitated; as the mind.
Silent; not sounded; having no sound; as a quiescent letter. Sow mow, with w quiescent; say, day, with y quiescent.
quiet: Still; being in a state of rest; now moving. Judges 16:2.
Still; free from alarm or disturbance; unmolested; as a quiet life. In his days the land was quiet ten years. 2 Chronicles 14:1.
Peaceable; not turbulent; not giving offense; not exciting controversy, disorder or trouble; mild; meek; contented. The ornament of a meek and quiet spirit.7 Peter 3:4; Thessalonians 4:11.
Calm; not agitated by wind; as a quiet sea or atmosphere.
quieter: The person or thing that quiets.
quieting: Reducing to rest or stillness; appeasing; tranquilizing.
quietism: Peace or tranquility of mind; apathy; dispassion; indisturbance; inaction. In history, quietism is the system of the quietists, who maintained that religion consists in the internal rest or recollection of the mind, employed in contemplating God and submitting to his will.
quietist: One of a sect of mystics, originated by Molino, a Spanish priest, who maintained the principles of quietism.
quietly: In a quiet state; without motion; in a state of rest; as, to lie or sit quietly.
Without tumult, alarm, dispute or disturbance; peaceably; as, to live quietly.
Calmly; without agitation or violent emotion; patiently. submit quietly to unavoidable evils.
quietness: Calm; tranquility; as the quietness of the ocean or atmosphere.
Freedom from agitation or emotion; calmness; coolness; as the quietness of the mind.
Freedom from disturbance, disorder or commotion; peace; tranquility; as the quietness of a city or state.
quietus: Rest; repose; death; hence, a final discharge or acquittance; that which silences claims.
quill: The large strong feather of a goose or other large fowl; used much for writing pens. Hence,
The instrument of writing; as the proper subject of his quill.
The spine or prickle of a porcupine.
A piece of small reed or other hollow plant, or which weavers wind the thread which forms the woof of cloth.
The instrument with which musicians strike the strings of certain instruments. To carry a good quill, to write well.
quillet: Subtilty; nicety; fraudulent distinction; petty cant.
quilt: A cover or garment made by putting wool, cotton or other substance between two cloths and sewing them together; as beds covered with magnificent quilts.
To stitch together two pieces of cloth with some soft and warm substance between them; as a quilted bedcover; a quilted coat.
To sew in the manner of a quilt.
quilted: Stitched together, as two pieces of cloth, with a soft substance between them.
quilting: Stitching together, as two cloths, with some soft substance between them.
The act of forming a quilt.
In New England, the act of quilting by a collection of females who bestow their labor gratuitously to aid a female friend, and conclude with an entertainment.
quinary: Consisting of five. Consisting of five; as a quinary number.
quinate: In botany, a quinate leaf is a sort of digitate leaf having five leaflets on a petiole.
quince: The fruit of the Pyrus cydonia, so named from Cydonia, a town of Crete, famous for abounding with this fruit. One species of this fruit is of an oblong shape, from which probably it has its French name.
The tree which produces the quince.
quince-tree: The tree which produces the quince.
quincuncial: Having the form of a quincunx.
quincunx: In gardening, the quincunx order is a plantation of trees disposed in a square, consisting of five trees, one at each corner and a fifth in the middle, thus :.:; which order repeated indefinitely, forms a regular grove or wood, which viewed by an angle of the square or parallelogram, presents equal or parallel alleys.
quindecagon: In geometry, a plain figure with fifteen sides and fifteen angles.
quindecemvir: In Roman history, one of a collection or body of fifteen magistrates, whose business was to preside over the sacrifices.
quindecemvirate: The body of fifteen magistrates, of their office.
quinia: In pharmacy, a substance prepared from yellow bark possessing in a concentrated form, the tonic virtues of the bark, and capable of forming salts with acids. One of these, the sulphate of quinine, is much employed in intermittent fevers and other diseases, where powerful tonics are required.
quinine: In pharmacy, a substance prepared from yellow bark possessing in a concentrated form, the tonic virtues of the bark, and capable of forming salts with acids. One of these, the sulphate of quinine, is much employed in intermittent fevers and other diseases, where powerful tonics are required.
quinquagesima: Quinquagesima Sunday, so called as being about the fiftieth day before Easter; Shrove Sunday.
quinquecapsular: In botany, having five capsules to a flower; as a quinquecapsular pericarp.
quinquefarious: In botany, opening into five parts.
quinquefid: In botany, five-cleft; cut into five segments with linear sinuses and straight margins; as a leaf.
quinquelobate: Five-lobed; divided to the middle into five distinct parts with convex margins.
quinquelobed: Five-lobed; divided to the middle into five distinct parts with convex margins.
quinquelocular: Five-celled; having five’ cells; as a pericarp.
quinquennial: Occurring once in five years, or lasting five years.
quinquepartite: Divided into five parts almost to the base.
quinquereme: A galley having five seats or rows’ of oars.
quinquevalve: Having five valves, as a pericarp.
quinquevalvular: Having five valves, as a pericarp.
quinquevir: One of an order of five priests in Rome.
quinsy: An inflammation of the throat; a species of angina which renders respiration difficult, or intercepts it.
An inflammation of the fauces, particularly of the tonsils.
quint: A set of sequence of five; as in piquet.
quintain: A post with a turning top.
quintal: A hundred pounds in weight; or a weight of that number of pounds; sometimes written and pronounced kentle.
quintessence: In alchimy, the fifth or last and highest essence of power in a natural body. Hence,

An extract from any thing, containing its virtues or most essential part in a small quantity: . Let there be light, said God; and forthwith light etherial, first of things, quintessence pure, sprung from the deep.
In chimistry, a preparation consisting of the essential oil of a vegetable substance, mixed and incorporated with spirit of wine.
The pure essential part of a thing.
quintile: The aspect of planets when distant from each other the fifth part of the zodiac, or 72 degrees.
quintin: An upright post on the top of which turned a cross piece, on one end of which was fixed a broad board, and on the other a sand bag. The play was to tilt on ride against the broad end with a lance, and pass without being struck by the sand bag behind.
quintuple: Fivefold; containing five times the amount.
quip: A smart sarcastic turn; a taunt; a severe retort.
To taunt; to treat with a sarcastic retort.
quire: A body of singers; a chorus.
The part of a church where the service is sung.
A collection of paper consisting of twenty four sheets, each having a single fold.
To sing in concert or chorus.
quirister: One that sings in concert; more generally, the leader of a quire, particularly in divine service; a chorister. But in America, this word is little used and vulgar. The word used is chorister.
quirk: Literally, a turn; a starting from the point or line; hence, an artful turn for evasion or subterfuge; a shift; a quibble; as the quirks of a pettifogger.
A fit or turn; a short paroxysm; as a quirk of joy or grief.
A smart taunt or retort. I may chance to have some odd quirks and remnants of wit broken on me.
quirkish: Consisting of quirks, turns, quibbles or artful evasions.
quirpele: The Indian ferret, an animal of the weasel kind.
quit: pret. and pp. quit or quitted.

To leave; to depart from, either temporarily or forever. It does not necessarily include the idea of abandoning, without a qualifying word. A man quits his house for an hour, or for a month. He quits his native country on a voyage or he quits it forever; he quits an employment with the intention of resuming it.
To free; to clear; to liberate; to discharge from. To quit you of this fear, you have already looked death in the face.
To carry through; to do or perform something to the end, so that nothing remains; to discharge or perform completely. Never a worthy prince a day did quit with greater hazard and with more renown.
To quit one’s self, reciprocally, to clear one’s self of incumbent duties by full performance. Samson hath quit himself like Samson. In this sense, acquit is generally used.
quitch-grass: Dog-grass; a species of grass which roots deeply and is not easily killed.
quitclaim: To release a claim by deed without covenants of warranty; to convey to another who hath some right in lands or tenements, all one’s right, title and interest in the estate, by relinquishing all claim to them. The words used in the instrument are, “A hath remised, released and forever quitclaimed all his right, title and interest to a certain estate.”
A deed of release; an instrument by which all claims to an estate are relinquished to another without any covenant or warranty, express or implied.
quite: Completely; wholly; entirely; totally; perfectly. The work is not quite done; the object is quite accomplished. He hath sold us and quite devoured also our money. Genesis 31:15. The same actions may be aimed at different ends, and arise from quite contrary principles.
quit-rent: A rent reserved in grants of land, by the payment of which the tenant is quieted or quit from all other service.
quits: An exclamation used when mutual demands are adjusted and the parties are even, each quit of the other.
quittance: Discharge from a debt or obligation; an acquittance.
quitter-bone: In farriery, a hard round swelling on the coronet, between the heel and the quarter, usually on the inside of the foot. R is the eighteenth letter of the English Alphabet, and an articulation sui generis, having little or no resemblance in pronunciation to any other letter. But from the position of the tongue in uttering it, it is commutable with I, into which letter it is changed in many words by the Spaniards and Portuguese, and some other nations; as I is also changed into r. It is numbered among the liquids and semi-vowels, and is sometimes called the canine letter. It is uttered with a guttural extrusion of the breath, and in some words, particularly at the end or after a labial and a dental letter, with a sort of quivering motion or slight jar of the tongue. Its English uses, which are uniform, may be understood by the customary pronunciation of rod, room, rose, bar, bare, barren,
quaternion: A file of four soldiers. Acts 12:4.
To divide into files or companies.
quatrain: A stanza of four lines rhyming alternately.
quaver: To shake the voice; to utter or form sound with rapid vibrations, as in singing; to sing with tremulous modulations of voice.
To tremble; to vibrate. The finger - moved with a quavering motion.
A shake or rapid vibration of the voice, or a shake on an instrument of music.
A note and measure of time in music, equal to half a crotchet or the eighth of a semibreve.
quavering: Shaking the voice or the sound of an instrument. quash’d. QUASH.
To crush; to subdue; as, to quash a rebellion. QUASH.
In law, to abate, annul, overthrow or make void; as, to quash an indictment. He pays judgment of the writ or declaration that the same may be quashed. QUASH.
The act of shaking the voice, or of making rapid vibrations of sound on an instrument of music.
quay: A key; a mole or wharf, constructed in harbors for securing vessels and receiving goods unladen or to be shipped on board.
queachy: Shaking; moving, yielding or trembling under the feet, as moist or boggy ground. The queachy fens. Godwin’s queachy sands.
quean: A worthless woman; a slut; a strumpet.
queasiness: s as z. Nausea; qualmishness; inclination to vomit.
queasy: Sick at the stomach; affected with nausea; inclined to vomit.
Causing nausea; as a queasy question.
queen: The consort of a king; a queen consort.
A woman who is the sovereign of a kingdom; a queen-regent; as Elizabeth, queen of England; Mary, queen of Scotland/\ .
The sovereign of a swarm of bees, or the female of the hive. A hive of bees cannot subsist without a queen. Queen of the meadows, meadow sweet, a plant of the genus Spiraea.
To play the queen; to act the part or character of a queen.
queen-apple: A kind of apple, so called.
queen-gold: A royal duty or revenue belonging to every queen of England during her marriage to the king.
queenly: Like a queen; becoming a queen; suitable to a queen.
queerly: In an odd or singular manner.
queest: A ring dove, a species of pigeon.
quell: To crush; to subdue; to cause to cease; as, to quell an insurrection or sedition.
To quiet; to allay; to reduce to peace; as, to quell the tumult of the soul.
To subdue; to reduce. This quell’d her pride.
quench: To extinguish; to put out; as, to quench flame.
To still; to quiet; to repress; as, to quench a passion or emotion.
To allay or extinguish; as, to quench thirst.
To check; to stifle; as, to quench the Spirit. 7 Thessalonians 5:19.
quenchable: That may be quenched or extinguished.
quenchless: That cannot be quenched or repressed; inextinguishable; as quenchless fire or fury.
quercitron: The bark of the yellow oak, used in dyeing.
quidnunc: One who is curious to know every thing that passes; one who knows or pretends to know all occurrences. Quid pro quo, in law, an equivalent; something given or done for another thing; mutual consideration and performance.
quiver: A case or sheath for arrows. Take the quiver and thy bow. Genesis 27:3.

To shake or tremble; to quake; to shudder; to shiver. This word expresses that tremulous motion of the body which proceeds from loss of heat or vigor. Thus persons quiver with fear or with cold. He quiver’d with his feet and lay for dead. And left the limbs still quiv’ring on the ground.
To play or be agitated with a tremulous motion. The green leaves quiver with the cooling wind. The lakes that quiver to the curling breeze.
quivered: Furnished with a quiver; as the quivered nymph.
Sheathed as in a quiver. - Whose quills stand quivered at his ear.
quivering: Trembling, as with cold or fear; moving with a tremulous agitation.
The act of shaking or trembling; agitation; as, to be seized with a quivering.
quixotic: Like Don Quixote; romantic to extravagance.
quixotism: Romantic and absurd notions; schemes or actions like those of Don Quixote, the hero of Cervantes.
quiz: To puzzle. Quo Warranto, in Law Latin, a writ brought before a proper tribunal, to inquire by what warrant a person or corporation exercises certain powers.
An enigma; a riddle or obscure question.
quob: To move, as the fetus in utero; to throb.
quodlibetarian: One who talks and disputes on any subject at pleasure.
quodlibetical: Not restrained to a particular subject; moved or discussed at pleasure for curiosity or entertainment.
quodlibetically: At pleasure; for curiosity; so as to be debated for entertainment.
quoif: To cover or dress with a coif.
quoin: An instrument to raise any thing; a wedge employed to raise cannon to a proper level, and for other purposes.
In architecture, the corner of a brick or stone wall.
quoit: A kind of horse shoe to be pitched or thrown at a fixed object in play. In common practice, a plain flat stone is used for this purpose.
In some authors, the discus of the ancients, thrown in trials of strength.
To throw quoits; to play at quoits.
quoll: An animal of New Holland, resembling the polecat.
quondam: used adjectively. Having been formerly; former; as a quondam king or friend.
quorum: A bench of justices, or such a number of officers or members as is competent by law or constitution to transact business; as a quorum of the house of representatives. A constitutional quorum was not present.
quota: A just part or share; or the share, part or proportion assigned to each. Each state was ordered to furnish its quota of troops.
quotation: The act of quoting or citing.
The passage quoted or cited; the part of a book or writing named, repeated or adduced as evidence or illustration.
In mercantile language, the naming of the price of commodities; or the price specified to a correspondent.
quote: To cite, as a passage from some author; to name, repeat or adduce a passage from an author or speaker, by way of authority or illustration; as, to quote a passage from Homer; to quote the words of Peter, or a passage of Paul’s writings; to quote chapter and verse.
In commerce, to name, as the price of an article.
A note upon an author. Obs.
quoter: One that cites the words of an author or speaker.
quoth: To say; to speak. This verb is defective, being used only in the first and third persons in the present and past tenses, as quoth I, quoth he, and the nominative always follows the verb. It is used only in ludicrous language, and has no variation for person, number or tense.
quotidian: A fever whose paroxysms return every day.
Daily; occurring or returning daily; as a quotidian fever.
quotient: In arithmetic, the number resulting from the division of one number by another, and showing how often a less number is contained in a greater. Thus 12/3 = 4. Here 4 is the quotient, showing that 3 is contained 4 times in 12. Or quotient is an expression denoting a certain part of a unit; as 3/4. R

R R is the eighteenth letter of the English Alphabet, and an articulation sui generis, having little or no resemblance in pronunciation to any other letter. But from the position of the tongue in uttering it, it is commutable with I, into which letter it is changed in many words by the Spaniards and Portuguese, and some other nations; as I is also changed into r. It is numbered among the liquids and semi-vowels, and is sometimes called the canine letter. It is uttered with a guttural extrusion of the breath, and in some words, particularly at the end or after a labial and a dental letter, with a sort of quivering motion or slight jar of the tongue. Its English uses, which are uniform, may be understood by the customary pronunciation of rod, room, rose, bar, bare, barren, disturb, catarrh, free, brad, pride, drip, dragIn words which we have received from the Greek language, we follow the
